summary:
Camillo Benso, Count of Cavour, served as Prime Minister of the Kingdom of Sardinia from 1852 to 1861 (with a brief interruption) and was the principal architect of [[event:2533|Italian Unification]]. Where Garibaldi provided the revolutionary sword, Cavour provided the diplomatic and political intelligence that directed Italian unification toward a constitutional monarchy under the House of Savoy rather than a republic or a confederation.

Cavour modernized Piedmont-Sardinia's economy and military, expanded its railway network, encouraged foreign investment, and built it into the most prosperous and constitutionally governed state in the Italian peninsula. He cultivated an alliance with France, sending Sardinian troops to the [[event:2547|Crimean War]] specifically to gain standing as an ally of the great powers — a shrewd investment that gave him access to Napoleon III and the Treaty of Plombières in 1858, which promised French military support against Austria in exchange for territorial concessions.

The Franco-Austrian War of 1859, triggered by Austrian ultimatums to Piedmont, secured Lombardy for Sardinia through French military assistance, though Napoleon III concluded a premature armistice at Villafranca that fell short of the full territorial program. Cavour resigned in protest but returned to office in 1860. He managed the critical challenge of directing Garibaldi's revolutionary conquests in the south toward the Sardinian monarchy rather than a republic, ordering Sardinian forces into the Papal States to intercept Garibaldi's northward march — an illegal international act that he accomplished with the connivance of Napoleon III.

Cavour died in June 1861, only three months after the proclamation of the Kingdom of Italy — which he had done more than any other single individual to bring about. He did not live to see Rome or Venice incorporated into the new kingdom, but his diplomatic framework made both eventual additions possible.
